IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of how to represent morphology in language models across typologically diverse languages, evaluating a wide range of subword units (characters, character trigrams, Morfessor, BPE, and manual morphemes) and composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN). Its finding that a previously unstudied combination—character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs—outperforms most alternatives, together with evidence that unsupervised segmentation (Morfessor, BPE) fails to match models using explicit/manual morphological analyses, is likely to change practitioners’ choice of subword representations and to spur research on better unsupervised morphological methods. Because the conclusions are empirically grounded across multiple languages and evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest neighbors), the work has strong impact within NLP and opens useful follow-up directions, though it is not outright transformative beyond the subfield. | B(4): The paper addresses an important, widely-relevant problem—how subword representations capture morphology for language modeling—and evaluates representations across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. 2015 data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). Its finding that a previously unstudied combination (character trigram representations with bi-LSTMs) outperforms most alternatives and that unsupervised subword methods (Morfessor, BPE) fail to match models with explicit/manual morphological analyses should change how researchers choose or design subword encodings. By highlighting the gap between unsupervised segmentation and gold morphological analyses, the work opens clear directions for improving unsupervised morphology and for integrating explicit morphological knowledge, making it likely to be built upon in multilingual and representation-learning research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of how to represent morphology in language models across typologically diverse languages, evaluating a wide range of subword units (characters, character trigrams, Morfessor, BPE, and manual morphemes) and composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN). Its finding that a previously unstudied combination—character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs—outperforms most alternatives, together with evidence that unsupervised segmentation (Morfessor, BPE) fails to match models using explicit/manual morphological analyses, is likely to change practitioners’ choice of subword representations and to spur research on better unsupervised morphological methods. Because the conclusions are empirically grounded across multiple languages and evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest neighbors), the work has strong impact within NLP and opens useful follow-up directions, though it is not outright transformative beyond the subfield.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important, widely-relevant problem—how subword representations capture morphology for language modeling—and evaluates representations across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. 2015 data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). Its finding that a previously unstudied combination (character trigram representations with bi-LSTMs) outperforms most alternatives and that unsupervised subword methods (Morfessor, BPE) fail to match models with explicit/manual morphological analyses should change how researchers choose or design subword encodings. By highlighting the gap between unsupervised segmentation and gold morphological analyses, the work opens clear directions for improving unsupervised morphology and for integrating explicit morphological knowledge, making it likely to be built upon in multilingual and representation-learning research.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper directly addresses core NLP/ML topics—subword representations, morphology, and language modeling—using standard model classes (LSTMs, CNNs), sensible composition functions, and expected evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. 2015 data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). The comparisons against established baselines (word embeddings, char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE) and use of human-annotated morphological analyses position it squarely for the NLP/ML audience. The problem formulation, experimental methodology, and evaluation standards match what top-tier venues expect, and the paper is well situated within the relevant research community given thorough related-work coverage. | B(5): This paper studies subword and morphological representations for LSTM language modeling across multiple languages, which is directly within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues (character/char-trigram/composed representations, bi-LSTMs/CNNs, perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) and compares against strong baselines (word models, char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE). The datasets (Ling et al. 2015, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA for Arabic) and thorough related-work coverage indicate it is aimed at the right audience and positioned appropriately for the community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper directly addresses core NLP/ML topics—subword representations, morphology, and language modeling—using standard model classes (LSTMs, CNNs), sensible composition functions, and expected evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. 2015 data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). The comparisons against established baselines (word embeddings, char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE) and use of human-annotated morphological analyses position it squarely for the NLP/ML audience. The problem formulation, experimental methodology, and evaluation standards match what top-tier venues expect, and the paper is well situated within the relevant research community given thorough related-work coverage.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper studies subword and morphological representations for LSTM language modeling across multiple languages, which is directly within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues (character/char-trigram/composed representations, bi-LSTMs/CNNs, perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) and compares against strong baselines (word models, char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE). The datasets (Ling et al. 2015, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA for Arabic) and thorough related-work coverage indicate it is aimed at the right audience and positioned appropriately for the community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (word models, char bi-LSTM from Ling et al. 2015, char CNN from Kim et al. 2016, Morfessor, and BPE) across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). Evaluations use consistent metrics (perplexity and nearest-neighbor cosine analyses) and reported hyperparameters, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when character or unsupervised subword methods fall short of models with manual morphological analyses. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the analysis contrasts the proposed character-trigram + bi-LSTM setting with competing approaches, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (standard word models, character bi-LSTM from Ling et al. 2015, character CNN from Kim et al. 2016, Morfessor, and BPE) and also evaluates models with access to manual morphological analyses (UD, MADAMIRA). Comparisons use the same evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor cosine) across shared datasets (Ling et al. data, Wikipedia, UD), hyperparameters are reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when manual morphological analyses outperform character/unsupervised methods. The paper also analyzes differences across composition functions and languages qualitatively and quantitatively and reports thorough related-work coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (word models, char bi-LSTM from Ling et al. 2015, char CNN from Kim et al. 2016, Morfessor, and BPE) across multiple languages and datasets (Ling et al. data, Wikipedia, UD, MADAMIRA). Evaluations use consistent metrics (perplexity and nearest-neighbor cosine analyses) and reported hyperparameters, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when character or unsupervised subword methods fall short of models with manual morphological analyses. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the analysis contrasts the proposed character-trigram + bi-LSTM setting with competing approaches, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (standard word models, character bi-LSTM from Ling et al. 2015, character CNN from Kim et al. 2016, Morfessor, and BPE) and also evaluates models with access to manual morphological analyses (UD, MADAMIRA). Comparisons use the same evaluation metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor cosine) across shared datasets (Ling et al. data, Wikipedia, UD), hyperparameters are reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when manual morphological analyses outperform character/unsupervised methods. The paper also analyzes differences across composition functions and languages qualitatively and quantitatively and reports thorough related-work coverage.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are generally well supported by cross-lingual LSTM language-model experiments comparing a wide set of sensible baselines (word, char-LSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor, BPE) and composition functions, with hyperparameters reported and evaluations on datasets including human-annotated UD morphologies for Czech/Russian. Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not report statistical significance testing, and the fixed-output-vocabulary experimental setup (no open-vocabulary prediction) could bias comparisons among subword methods. Also, the use of MADAMIRA (an automatic analyzer) for Arabic versus manual annotations in other languages introduces a potential inconsistency when claiming superiority of explicit/manual morphological analyses, but these are minor and do not overturn the presented experimental evidence. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by cross-lingual experiments comparing a comprehensive set of baselines (word, character bi-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE) using standard metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) and human/manual morphological analyses (UD, MADAMIRA). There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the experimental setup reports hyperparameters and covers relevant baselines, which indicates a generally sound methodology. Minor concerns remain—most notably the fixed output-vocabulary restriction (which can affect word-level prediction comparisons) and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing in the summary—but these do not substantially undermine the paper’s main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are generally well supported by cross-lingual LSTM language-model experiments comparing a wide set of sensible baselines (word, char-LSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor, BPE) and composition functions, with hyperparameters reported and evaluations on datasets including human-annotated UD morphologies for Czech/Russian. Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not report statistical significance testing, and the fixed-output-vocabulary experimental setup (no open-vocabulary prediction) could bias comparisons among subword methods. Also, the use of MADAMIRA (an automatic analyzer) for Arabic versus manual annotations in other languages introduces a potential inconsistency when claiming superiority of explicit/manual morphological analyses, but these are minor and do not overturn the presented experimental evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by cross-lingual experiments comparing a comprehensive set of baselines (word, character bi-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE) using standard metrics (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) and human/manual morphological analyses (UD, MADAMIRA). There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the experimental setup reports hyperparameters and covers relevant baselines, which indicates a generally sound methodology. Minor concerns remain—most notably the fixed output-vocabulary restriction (which can affect word-level prediction comparisons) and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing in the summary—but these do not substantially undermine the paper’s main experimental conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad and cover multiple languages and morphological typologies, a variety of subword units (chars, char trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), and composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN), evaluated with relevant metrics (perplexity, nearest neighbors), which supports the main claims. Ablative comparisons between composition functions are reported and hyperparameters are provided, and related work appears thoroughly covered. The paper discusses limitations and negative results (e.g., character and unsupervised subword methods falling short of manual morphological analyses, and the fixed output-vocabulary constraint). A minor gap is reproducibility and openness: code is not mentioned, and the fixed-vocabulary setup limits claims about open-vocabulary modeling, so I rate it solid but with small shortcomings. | B(4): The experiments are broad and well-structured: multiple languages and typologies, several subword segmentation methods (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), multiple composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN), and comparisons to strong baselines using perplexity and nearest-neighbor analyses. Ablations/sensitivity analyses appear to be included via the different composition and segmentation choices, and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is described as thorough. Limitations and negative results are explicitly discussed (e.g., fixed output vocabulary, unsupervised morphology shortcomings), but reproducibility is somewhat weakened by no mention of released code and the fixed-vocabulary evaluation choice.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broad and cover multiple languages and morphological typologies, a variety of subword units (chars, char trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), and composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN), evaluated with relevant metrics (perplexity, nearest neighbors), which supports the main claims. Ablative comparisons between composition functions are reported and hyperparameters are provided, and related work appears thoroughly covered. The paper discusses limitations and negative results (e.g., character and unsupervised subword methods falling short of manual morphological analyses, and the fixed output-vocabulary constraint). A minor gap is reproducibility and openness: code is not mentioned, and the fixed-vocabulary setup limits claims about open-vocabulary modeling, so I rate it solid but with small shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad and well-structured: multiple languages and typologies, several subword segmentation methods (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), multiple composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN), and comparisons to strong baselines using perplexity and nearest-neighbor analyses. Ablations/sensitivity analyses appear to be included via the different composition and segmentation choices, and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is described as thorough. Limitations and negative results are explicitly discussed (e.g., fixed output vocabulary, unsupervised morphology shortcomings), but reproducibility is somewhat weakened by no mention of released code and the fixed-vocabulary evaluation choice.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper mainly performs a systematic empirical study combining existing components (characters, character trigrams, Morfessor, BPE) with known composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN) and identifies a previously unstudied combination (character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs) that performs best. The problem (subword representations and morphology for language modeling) is well-studied, and the authors clearly differentiate their contributions via broad multilingual evaluations and comparison to manual morphological analyses. However, the work is largely an incremental empirical advance rather than proposing a fundamentally new method or theory, though its negative finding about unsupervised segmentation vs. manual morphology is informative to experts. | B(4): The paper does not introduce a brand-new architecture but provides a clear, novel empirical perspective by systematically evaluating combinations of subword units (characters, character trigrams, morphs from Morfessor/BPE) and composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN) across languages and morphological typologies. It identifies a previously unstudied and effective setting (character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs) and convincingly differentiates its findings from prior work (e.g., character bi-LSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor/BPE baselines). These results—particularly that unsupervised subword segmentation fails to match explicit/manual morphological analyses—would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts, making the contribution a notable empirical advance rather than a purely incremental combination of known methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper mainly performs a systematic empirical study combining existing components (characters, character trigrams, Morfessor, BPE) with known composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN) and identifies a previously unstudied combination (character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs) that performs best. The problem (subword representations and morphology for language modeling) is well-studied, and the authors clearly differentiate their contributions via broad multilingual evaluations and comparison to manual morphological analyses. However, the work is largely an incremental empirical advance rather than proposing a fundamentally new method or theory, though its negative finding about unsupervised segmentation vs. manual morphology is informative to experts.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and logical (intro → methods evaluating subword composition functions → experiments across multiple languages → conclusion), and the main contributions are stated early (claims about character trigram + bi-LSTM and limits of unsupervised morphology). Writing quality is reported as good, related work coverage thorough, and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics like perplexity and cosine-similarity, and hyperparameters) are provided, which supports clear communication. Minor presentation uncertainties remain from the summary — notation consistency and the effectiveness of figures/tables/examples are not explicitly confirmed — so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear experimental flow (methods -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions and claims early (character trigram + bi-LSTM result; limits of unsupervised morph). Notation and methodological descriptions seem consistent and complete (hyperparameters reported, thorough related work), and the use of perplexity and nearest-neighbor analyses across multiple datasets and baselines makes the results easy to follow. Writing quality is rated good in the summary, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit mention of figures/tables effectiveness or code availability) are not reported in the summary, so I reserve one point for small, unspecified presentation gaps.

main_claims: Character representations are effective across many languages for language modeling.
A previously unstudied combination—character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs—outperforms most other tested settings.

Character-level or unsupervised subword segmentation does not match the predictive accuracy of models with access to explicit (manual) morphological analyses; current unsupervised morphological analysis methods are inadequate substitutes.
_summary.methodology: They evaluate LSTM language models that compute word representations by composing subword units (characters, character trigrams, morphs from Morfessor or BPE) using different composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN), and compare models via perplexity and qualitative nearest-neighbor analyses across languages and morphological typologies.
datasets: Datasets from Ling et al. (2015) for English and Turkish
Universal Dependencies (UD) datasets for Czech and Russian (human-annotated morphological analyses)
Arabic analyses via MADAMIRA (Pasha et al., 2014)
baselines: Morph-based segmentation via Morfessor (Smit et al., 2014)
limitations: Character models do not match the predictive accuracy of models with explicit/manual morphological analyses.
Unsupervised morphological segmentation (e.g., Morfessor, BPE) is an inadequate substitute for manual morphological analysis.
Model predicts only words from a fixed output vocabulary (no open-vocabulary prediction in this work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - subword representations, morphology, and language modeling
